\title{Joint Characterization of Foregrounds and Propagation Effects in Fast Radio Bursts Co-detected by CHIME/FRB and DSA-110}

% Author / affiliation block lives in auth.tex for reuse and clean diffs.
\input{auth.tex}

\begin{abstract}
% TODO(abstract): bracketed slots below are placeholders for withheld results;
% fill each only after the producing analysis clears re-validation, then delete
% this comment.
Fast radio bursts (FRBs) probe ionized gas on Galactic, circumgalactic,
intergalactic, and host-galaxy scales through their dispersion and scattering
signatures. Single-band data limit the interpretation of both observables: a
lone band cannot separate the scattering time from its frequency scaling, and
the dispersion measure (DM) alone does not reveal how the electron column is
divided among the media along the sightline. Here we analyze twelve FRBs
co-detected by CHIME/FRB ($0.4$--$0.8$\,GHz) and DSA-110 ($\sim$1.4\,GHz)
between February 2022 and February 2024. All twelve pass timing and positional
association tests with chance-coincidence probabilities $P_{\rm cc}<10^{-6}$
(the eight pairs with pre-specified DM-conditioned tests pass those as well),
and new phase-coherence measurements constrain the CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 DMs
independently for every event. The two bands agree to within
$0.12\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$; we adopt the sharper CHIME/FRB measurement for
each burst and retain DSA-110 as an independent cross-check. The
$\sim$1\,GHz frequency baseline between the two facilities breaks the
degeneracy between scattering time and frequency scaling that limits any
single-band measurement. Because the pulse-broadening shape and the scaling
index $\alpha$ both follow from the same turbulence spectrum, we model them
jointly through the spectral index $\beta$ of the electron-density fluctuation
spectrum, rather than fitting a fixed pulse-broadening shape with $\alpha$
free---a combination that is physically inconsistent and biases the inferred
scaling.
% [SLOT: joint two-band scattering results---validated $\tau_{1\,\mathrm{GHz}}$,
% $\beta$, and multiplicity statements.]
A foreground census of all twelve sightlines, drawing on DESI Legacy, GLADE+,
SDSS, and cluster catalogs---deduplicated across catalogs, with vetted
empirical stellar masses for every candidate---finds a single sightline
(FRB~20230307A) that passes within $R_{500}$ of a foreground cluster
($b/R_{500}=0.83$). For that crossing, a two-phase modified-NFW model predicts
an intracluster dispersion-measure column of order
$180\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$, uncertain at the factor-of-two level
($\approx100$--$560\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ across the profile choice and
cluster-parameter systematics). The confirmed foreground galaxies on this and
three further sightlines sit in the outer circumgalactic medium
($b/R_{\rm vir}\approx0.46$--$0.96$) and contribute columns of
$\approx6$--$76\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ each.
A per-sightline dispersion-measure budget over that census finds the
intervening column dominated by the cluster-crossing sightline
($243\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$), with a secondary contribution toward
FRB~20240229A ($117\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ from two circumgalactic halos).
The forward-modeled host contributions are broad and asymmetric, with
posterior medians spanning $\approx-10$ to $+170\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$.
Within the uncertainties, every redshift-known sightline is consistent with
the host supplying a minority of the extragalactic column, which is typically
dominated by the cosmological term; the one marginally negative host
posterior (FRB~20220310F, $z=0.48$) reflects sightline-to-sightline scatter
about the Macquart relation rather than an unphysical host term. These
measurements sharpen the attribution of dispersion and scattering along FRB
sightlines and inform models of the circumgalactic and intergalactic media.
% [SLOT: scintillation constraints and screen attribution.]
% [SLOT: band-restricted burst energies.]
% [SLOT: one-sentence physical conclusion tying scattering attribution to the
% foreground census.]
\end{abstract}

\keywords{Radio transient sources (2008) --- Radio bursts (1339)
--- Interstellar scattering (854) --- Intergalactic medium (813)
--- Circumgalactic medium (1879)}

\section*{Data Availability}
\phantomsection
\label{sec:data-availability}
% TODO(data-availability): before submission: (i) make the pipeline repo
% public and pin the release tag used for the accepted version; (ii) state
% where the dynamic spectra and fit products live and under what access
% policy.
The dynamic spectra, fit products, and foreground-census tables supporting
this work will be made available upon publication. The reduction and fitting
pipeline is publicly available at
\url{https://github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS}; the accepted version of
this manuscript corresponds to a tagged release of that repository. The
manuscript source repository includes the adopted per-burst DM catalog, the
full CHIME/FRB and DSA-110 fit results, per-event diagnostic figures, injection
tests, input hashes, and an implementation snapshot under
\texttt{analysis/dm-joint-phase-v2/}.

\facilities{CHIME, OVRO:DSA-110}

\software{\texttt{astropy} \citep{astropy2013, astropy2018, astropy2022},
\texttt{dynesty} \citep{Speagle2020}, \texttt{numpy} \citep{Harris2020numpy},
\texttt{scipy} \citep{Virtanen2020scipy},
\texttt{matplotlib} \citep{Hunter2007}, \texttt{pygedm} \citep{Price2021} (NE2001/YMW16),
\texttt{mwprop} \citep{Ocker2026} (NE2025), and the open-source co-detection
reduction and fitting pipeline
(\url{https://github.com/jakobtfaber/dsa110-FLITS}).
}
